name: Validation Engine
tagline: A read-only rules engine that flags workbook defects human review misses — and can never introduce one.
plain_summary: The Validation Engine opens a finished Excel workbook read-only and runs six independent checks over it: whether the closing-surplus total is formula-driven rather than typed over, whether the trial balance ties out, whether stale review notes were left behind, whether the audit lineage flows inputs to formulas, whether draft-only MIN/MAX cap logic was ever replaced, and whether the sibling JSON export still agrees with the workbook. Each check yields a PASS, FAIL, or FLAG finding with a Sheet!Cell location, and the findings roll up to one verdict — PASS, REVIEW, or FAIL — plus a matching process exit code. It ships with a seeded synthetic generator so it runs end-to-end on fully fictional data, and it never writes to the file it audits.
marketing_thesis: Every count on this page is a command, not a claim. The six checks, the seven-workbook demo corpus, and the pinned test total each re-derive from the public repository.
layers_intro: Each layer is an independent stage of the read-only pipeline. Select a layer for its plain-terms and engineering detail.

layers.label: Verdict & Reporting
layers.plain: Rolls every finding up into one verdict — PASS, REVIEW, or FAIL — prints the findings, and writes the markdown and JSON reports. The verdict also becomes the process exit code, so a script or CI can act on it.
layers.engineering: overall_verdict() takes the worst per-workbook Verdict; build_json_report() / build_markdown_report() serialise the run; cli.main() maps the verdict to an exit code via _EXIT_BY_VERDICT (PASS 0, REVIEW 1, FAIL 2; usage error 3).

layers.label: Registry Runner
layers.plain: For each workbook, runs the whole rule registry in order and collects the findings into one report — the same rules, in the same order, every time.
layers.engineering: validate_workbook() iterates REGISTRY of (rule_id, fn) pairs and extends WorkbookReport.findings; validate_folder() applies it to every *.xlsx in a folder, sorted, skipping ~$ lock files.

layers.label: Rule Registry (6 checks)
layers.plain: The six independent checks that inspect the cells: expected-formula, debit/credit balance, forbidden text, lineage direction, leftover cap logic, and JSON tie-out. Adding a rule is one decorated function.
layers.engineering: The @check(rule_id) decorator appends each (WorkbookContext) -> list[Finding] function to REGISTRY. Rule configuration (EXPECTED_FORMULA_CELLS, FORBIDDEN_TEXT, trial-balance columns, evidence/detail cells) stays declarative at module top.

layers.label: Workbook Context (dual read-only load)
layers.plain: Loads each workbook twice read-only — once to see the formulas, once to see the cached values — and attaches the sibling JSON export if present. This is what makes the engine incapable of writing to the audited file.
layers.engineering: _load_context() calls load_workbook(read_only=True) with data_only=False and data_only=True; WorkbookContext exposes formula(), value(), iter_formula_cells(), and the parsed json_data from <stem>.json.

layers.label: Seeded Synthetic Corpus
layers.plain: A generator that writes a fully fictional set of workbooks — one clean, and one per rule each carrying a single planted defect — so the engine runs end-to-end with no real files and every rule has a live test case.
layers.engineering: generate_corpus() seeds the stdlib random module with SEED=20240614, builds four-tab workbooks (Surplus-Detail / Summary / Trial-Balance / Evidence) for fictional entities, plants one Defect per DEFECTS entry, and writes each .xlsx plus a .json sibling.

blocks.label: Seeded generator
blocks.plain: Writes fictional workbooks and JSON exports to ./samples — one clean and one per planted defect.
blocks.engineering: generate.generate_corpus(out_dir, seed=20240614); wipes stale *.xlsx / *.json first, then writes the clean plus six defect workbooks with sibling .json.

blocks.label: Dual read-only load
blocks.plain: Opens each workbook twice read-only (formulas and cached values) and attaches the sibling JSON export.
blocks.engineering: engine._load_context(): load_workbook(read_only=True) twice (data_only False / True) plus json.loads(<stem>.json), wrapped in WorkbookContext.

blocks.label: expected_formula
blocks.plain: Checks that cells which should be formula-driven hold a formula, not a typed-in number.
blocks.engineering: check_expected_formula() over EXPECTED_FORMULA_CELLS; a literal is a FAIL.

blocks.label: debit_credit_balance
blocks.plain: Sums the trial-balance debit and credit columns and confirms they tie out.
blocks.engineering: check_debit_credit_balance() sums TB_DEBIT_COL / TB_CREDIT_COL from cached values; |diff| >= 1e-6 is a FAIL.

blocks.label: forbidden_text
blocks.plain: Scans every cell for stale review notes and internal / tool words (TODO, 'reviewer decision', tool names).
blocks.engineering: check_forbidden_text() substring-matches FORBIDDEN_TEXT over iter_formula_cells(); a hit is a FLAG.

blocks.label: lineage_direction
blocks.plain: Confirms evidence cells are raw inputs (literals) and detail drivers are formulas; either inversion flags.
blocks.engineering: check_lineage_direction() over EVIDENCE_INPUT_CELLS and DETAIL_DRIVER_CELLS; an inversion is a FLAG.

blocks.label: cap_logic_leftover
blocks.plain: Detects leftover draft MIN/MAX cap logic that should have been replaced before final.
blocks.engineering: check_cap_logic_leftover() applies a case-insensitive MIN/MAX open-paren regex over formula cells; a match is a FLAG.

blocks.label: json_tieout
blocks.plain: Cross-checks the sibling JSON export's closing_surplus against the workbook's resolved value.
blocks.engineering: check_json_tieout() compares json_data['closing_surplus'] to _closing_surplus_value(); a mismatch is a FAIL, an unresolvable value a FLAG.

blocks.label: Verdict roll-up
blocks.plain: Collapses all findings for a workbook, and then the whole run, into one verdict — worst wins.
blocks.engineering: WorkbookReport.verdict (any FAIL -> FAIL, else any FLAG -> REVIEW, else PASS), then overall_verdict() over all reports.

blocks.label: Reports + exit code
blocks.plain: Emits the markdown and JSON reports and returns an exit code encoding the verdict.
blocks.engineering: build_markdown_report() / build_json_report() write the files; cli.main() returns _EXIT_BY_VERDICT[overall] (0 / 1 / 2), or 3 on a usage error.

spec_strip.label: Tests
spec_strip.source: python -m pytest --collect-only -q (audit-automation/); verified 2026-07-20 and pinned in site-datasheets/counts.json
spec_strip.plain: the checks that pin this engine's behavior

spec_strip.label: Rules in registry
spec_strip.plain: six independent checks run over every workbook

spec_strip.label: Verdict states
spec_strip.plain: the worst finding wins the workbook

spec_strip.label: Read passes / workbook
spec_strip.plain: opened twice read-only — formulas and cached values — never written

spec_strip.label: Demo corpus
spec_strip.plain: one clean plus one per rule, each with a single planted defect

problem_statement: Large, formula-heavy financial workbooks must be checked before sign-off, and the errors that survive human review are the subtle ones: a total hardcoded on top of its formula, a trial balance off by a plug, a leftover 'reviewer decision' note, inverted lineage where an evidence input holds a formula, a draft MIN/MAX cap that was never removed, or a published JSON that no longer matches the workbook it came from. Checking these by eye across many near-identical workbooks is slow and error-prone, and asking an LLM 'did you fix everything?' returns a confident 'yes' whether or not it is true. This engine replaces that with a deterministic, read-only registry of checks that runs identically every time and cannot alter the audited file.

scenarios.title: A hardcoded total slips past review
scenarios.narrative: Maple Fund LP's closing surplus was typed in as the literal 900 instead of the formula =B3+B4+B5. expected_formula fires FAIL at Surplus-Detail!B6 ('expected a formula but found hardcoded value: 900'), lineage_direction adds a FLAG on the same cell, and the workbook verdict is FAIL.

scenarios.title: A trial balance off by a plug
scenarios.narrative: Birchwood Op Co's trial balance no longer ties out. debit_credit_balance sums the columns and reports FAIL at Trial-Balance!B:C ('debit=885 vs credit=875, diff=10'), driving the workbook to FAIL.

scenarios.title: A published JSON disagrees with the workbook
scenarios.narrative: Demo Holdings LLC's sibling JSON export carries closing_surplus=1725 while the workbook resolves to 1700. json_tieout fires FAIL at json:closing_surplus, catching a published figure that drifted from its source.

instruction_set.operation: zero-arg quickstart: regenerate the fictional corpus into ./samples, validate it, and write both reports
instruction_set.output: generation line, per-workbook findings, 'Overall verdict: FAIL (7 workbook(s))'

instruction_set.operation: validate every .xlsx in a folder (read-only) and print findings
write the seeded fictional workbooks + JSON exports into ./samples
(re)generate the corpus, validate, and write the structured JSON + markdown reports
run the test suite (defects caught, clean passes, report shape, read-only, determinism)

benchmarks.label: Demo workbooks validated
benchmarks.plain: the committed example run validates seven workbooks and returns overall FAIL

benchmarks.label: Planted defects caught
benchmarks.plain: one defect per rule is planted and each is caught by its own rule; the clean workbook passes

benchmarks.label: Rules in registry
benchmarks.plain: six checks run over every workbook, each with a fixed severity

benchmarks.label: Engine tests
benchmarks.source: python -m pytest --collect-only -q (audit-automation/); verified 2026-07-20 and pinned in site-datasheets/counts.json

verdict_map.severity: Any FAIL finding (expected_formula, debit_credit_balance, or a json_tieout mismatch)
verdict_map.action: exit code 2 — a hard defect is present; block sign-off

verdict_map.severity: One or more FLAG findings and no FAIL (forbidden_text, lineage_direction, cap_logic_leftover, or an unresolvable json_tieout)
verdict_map.action: exit code 1 — human review before sign-off

guarantees: Strictly read-only: every workbook is opened with read_only=True (twice) and never written, so the engine cannot introduce a defect — the audited file is byte-identical before and after, asserted by test_readonly_and_determinism.py.
Deterministic: a fixed order over REGISTRY plus a fixed generator seed (SEED=20240614) means identical inputs always yield identical findings in identical order.
Offline: the only third-party dependencies are openpyxl and pytest; synthetic data uses the stdlib random module — no network and no external services.
Refuses rather than fudges: json_tieout emits a FLAG, not a fabricated comparison, when the JSON has no closing_surplus key or the workbook value cannot be resolved.
Fail-safe roll-up: any single FAIL forces the whole run to FAIL and exit code 2 — a defect cannot be diluted by surrounding passes.
control_characteristics.plain: The engine only reports; it never writes to, approves, or signs off the audited file. The PASS / REVIEW / FAIL verdict and its exit code (0 / 1 / 2, or 3 for a usage error) are the handoff a human reviewer or CI pipeline consumes to decide whether to proceed.

limits.statement: Never writes to an audited workbook — it opens files read-only (twice) and can only report, never introduce or fix a defect.
Refuses to guess a JSON tie-out: if the export has no closing_surplus key or the workbook value cannot be resolved, it emits a FLAG rather than fabricating a pass or fail comparison.
Only checks a fixed rule set against a fixed workbook schema (Surplus-Detail / Summary / Trial-Balance / Evidence); when an expected sheet is absent it skips rather than inventing findings.

media.poster_alt: Isometric brand animation: a stack of sheets labeled 'Finished workbook' at left; a translucent glass bracket labeled 'Validation Engine — read-only, never writes' passing over a workbook at center with a blue scan glow; and an output stack at right tagged with three verdict chips — PASS (green), REVIEW (grey), FAIL (amber).
media.caption: Animated overview of the read-only validation pass on fictional seeded data; run the commands above to reproduce.

meta.description: Validation Engine (SFS-E04-VAL) datasheet — a deterministic, read-only rules engine that runs six independent checks over finished .xlsx workbooks and their JSON exports, rolling PASS/REVIEW/FAIL findings up to one verdict and exit code; every figure re-derives from the public repository.
